comments.title: High Energy
comments.content:
I've made meditation a regular practice for a couple years and have lately had some interesting results. As a bit of background, I learned the microcosmic orbit technique many years ago and ever since I've practiced circulating energy in that way, along with keeping aware of my chakras. Apart from these practices my approach tends more Western than Eastern, using discursive meditation combined with divination and banishing rituals.

Recently I've had a few experiences of entering a high-energy state. When this happens I feel the presence of intense warm energy enveloping me, and within my torso the energy circulates in a microcosmic orbit without my making any effort to produce the effect. My breathing becomes naturally synchronized with the orbit and my chakras feel so distinct that they almost register to me as physical parts of my body. Again, in the high-energy state this awareness of circulation is automatic and doesn't require deliberate focus. While in this state, the effects of meditation and other spiritual practices are greatly boosted, and I generally feel enhanced in every way: stronger, more perceptive and with a clearer mind.

This state is naturally quite enjoyable to be in, and its experiences have also brought me understanding of things that were troubling before. For example, I realized that some of my impulsive overeating habits stemmed from an energetic imbalance and I better understood how to avoid falling into that pattern. When I've achieved the high-energy state it tends to last for the better part of a day at full intensity, and then gradually fades over the following half day or so.

Are you familiar with such states and any sources on how to cultivate and make the best use of this phenomenon? My ability to reach this state is definitely helped by meditation and energetic work but the exact requirements for it have been elusive to me. It occurs now and then as a matter of course in my practices but if I try to find and meet criteria to enter the state it doesn't work. I don't think this is a kundalini rising because it doesn't fulfill the criteria as far as I've read. I don't get a sense that the energy moves up from the base of the spine and I've never felt my health was in any danger from it, quite the opposite. I doubt it's possible to be like this all the time but if it can be done in a regular, predictable way it would be great. Thanks for reading.

replies.title: Re: High Energy
replies.content: Fascinating. I'm familiar with the microcosmic orbit and the other Taoist energy circulation methods but they're not something I've practiced, nor does any of my training address how to work with it. You may need to find someone with a background in Taoist energy work or qigong to find an answer to that.

replies.title: Re: High Energy
replies.content:
I haven't experienced what you have but am familiar with the microcosmic orbit. Do you do any of the other practices that extend from the micocosmic orbit (larger orbit, belt channels, thrusting channels etc)?
Don't know, but first thought was the possibility that your practice has 'developed organically/naturally' - i.e. the energetic metaphors are just patterns and it is not so much the particular 'system' but systems lay the groundwork for potential of development and that alongwith your other practices you have 'raised' your energy potential.

When I was taught the basic orbit, after the clearing of those channels and wider flows, one of the next steps was to begin working on the emotional energies through the fusion of the five elemental processes which led to the condensing and raising of the 'pearl' of energy on the way to developing the light body.
Essentially - cleanse, strengthen, develop, condense and then raise consciousness beyond the physical

I should point out that my practice became a hybrid between east and west with emphasis not on the idea of physical immortality but the development of the 'rarefied bodies' so is a bit of a 'Heath Robinson device' that seems to work for me but I'm not selling/recommending ideas to anyone!

I learnt but moved away (many years ago) from the chi kung with forced breathing and visualisation as it seemed contrary trying to force what should not be forced, and moved towards gentler methods and then to letting breath do its own thing. Breath control has a place, but is a starting point not continual requirement.

Wong Kiew Kit's Art of Wisdom I think of as cosmos breathing and a natural extension from the orbits and other flows and a method of aiming for the grace of intuition; the raising of the light body has parallels with Mr Greer's Three Cauldrons exercise.

I haven't taught for a long time as my methods have veered to the experimental, but your experience sounds to me as though you are very ready to move/develop your practice further... how exactly depends on your individual circumstances.

40 years ago one of the practices Mantak Chia taught was something he called practising death (raising the condensed energy body beyond the physical) and I found that a very good exercise, but the Taoist symbology did not suit me and my path diverged towards something like you said - a blend of east and west with emphasis on 'consciousness' rather than 'personal power'.

I've drifted too far from the Taoist practices to offer advice, mainly I'd ask - what energy meditations do you do beyond the microcosmic orbit? It sounds like you raised your energy level to the extent you have surplus to apply further in personal development, healing or whatever.

[earthworm]

replies.title: Re: High Energy
replies.content:
PS
"It sounds like you raised your energy level to the extent you have surplus to apply further in personal development, healing or whatever."

That is not suggesting that you use/give your own energy in healing, rather, that your raised energy state means you can better connect to the 'source' of healing energy and the ability to channel or direct that... very different things.

[earthworm]

replies.title: Re: Amber
replies.content: Every herb or stone has two sets of properties. The first set come from its elemental and planetary correspondences. The second is unique to the herb or stone and can't necessarily be related to the elements or the planets. This is a great example. Amber's property of assisting babies while they're teething has no obvious relationship to its solar and fiery qualities -- it's just something additional that amber happens to have.

replies.content:

Reading about it, it kind of struck me that there might be a marketing reason behind it, that is why I ask about the tradition. For instance red corals are known to have been used since at least the Roman times.

replies.title: Re: Amber

Two forces - below and above - form an axis which provides the basic framework of every individuals existence. Laws or principles that are provided by the cosmos, as well as the implementation of these through the earth which provides adds its own laws and substance. This is what is more or less the same in every human. The third force as the "force to become", in the human case an ideal, a role model to shape the forces of lunar current which is born in us, if you will.

comments.title: Mindset for change
comments.content:
Beginning with learning a magical curriculum, what is the best mindset to enter with? Obviously change cannot take place when one is stuck in old patterns and beliefs. Yet the aspirant does not really know the way yet - what they might imagine as the destination is probably different than the actual thing.

What kind of approach or state of mind can an aspirant adopt to make the most out of their magical training?

Thanks for your kindness and patience in answering us every week. May you be blessed.

replies.title: Re: Mindset for change
replies.content: The best mindset is simply a willingness to trust whatever system you're using -- to study what you're asked to study and practice what you're asked to practice, without trying to tinker with it or tell yourself that you know better than the people who designed it. (In the vast majority of cases, you don't.) Especially if you don't have any previous experience with magic, the nature of the work and the destinations to be reached are as incomprehensible as managing a sailboat would be to someone who'd never encountered wind or open water, so all you can do is keep an open mind and trust the teachings.

comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I love that meme! As I'm heading into a break from work and studying, I'm prepping for the CGD Druid grade and have a query I hope you can help with. I'm wondering why 6 ounces of each herb is required to construct the Serpent's Egg (one of my Bardic Grade herbs is chamomile, which will be verrry bulky at that weight) and not 2, 3 or 4, say? I'm having trouble picturing the volume of ash that will be produced from the five herbs in total and how big the interior of my "egg" will have to be.

I've also started researching a potential book about the esoteric side of food (discussed here on MM a month or so back) and spent an entertaining few hours falling down an archive.org rabbit hole of "Books of secrets" and how Paracelsus changed the Middle Ages diet. Do you think it's worth pitching the idea/a detailed outline first (I reckon Aeon would be a great fit) to gauge interest or to knuckle down and start compiling it?

With thanks as always for your time and wisdom, and I hope your mood lifts soon.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The goal is to make sure that no matter what happens in the process, you end up with enough raw material. You can use quite a bit less if you're careful and nothing goes wrong. Remember, though, that the herbs will compact quite a bit when calcined!

2) Write it first. If you don't have a past track record as a published author, publishers will want to be sure that you can actually finish a manuscript before giving you a contract.

replies.content: Thank you! Start with the container you intend to use as the core of your Druid's Egg. Use your previous experience with calcining herbs to judge how much total herb you'll be using, divide as needed (given the number of herbs you'll be using) and add a fudge factor just in case.

comments.title: Prayer to St. Michael
comments.content:
Hi JMG.
Thank you for being here, "a little depressed' and all. Last week, it was discussed here that some Christian prayers include curses. I'm wondering about the Prayer to St. Michael. Isn't "cast into hell Satan, and all the evil spirits" actually a curse?

replies.title: Re: Prayer to St. Michael
replies.content: Nah, you're just telling them to go back home where they belong.

replies.title: Re: Prayer to St. Michael
replies.content:
Insofar as it matters how it was worded in the original language, the original Latin was "Satanam aliosque spiritus malignos [...] in infernum detrude." "Detrude" has a range of meanings but they are more focused on the present location that something is being pushed away from than on any particular location that something is being pushed into, and on breaking a connection rather than creating one.

https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/detrudo

The entire formula actually ends with that verb plus the "amen". There might prove to be a net advantage in effectiveness from giving up the orthopraxy of reciting the standard English translation and instead rearranging the clauses to better match the order of the Latin, at least so as to put the core verb phrase at the climax.

replies.title: Re: Prayer to St. Michael
replies.content: Interesting. Yet "in infernum" as an accusative construction, unless I misread it, adds in the sense of being pushed into someplace.

comments.title: Meditation, energy centers, complexes
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I hope your depression passes!

1. What actually happens when your mind drifts off into distraction in occult terms? This is prompted by W.E. replies.title: Re: The Lunar Current

The largest institutional bodies in the Abrahamic faiths insist that there's only one god. The Bible itself contradicts them. Here's the first line of Psalm 128:

"I will praise thee with my whole heart: before the gods will I sing praise unto thee."

Here's what Jephthah, one of the judges of Israel, said to the Ammonites in Judges 11:24:

"Wilt not thou possess that which Chemosh thy god giveth thee to possess? So whomsoever the LORD our God shall drive out from before us, them will we possess."

Official Christian and Jewish commentators have spent centuries arguing that these and other passages don't mean what they obviously do mean. They're wrong, but try telling them that!

Not only that but archaeology shows us that the Jews were still polytheists to some degree as late as the 400s BC, via the Elephantine documents and some Babylonian inscriptions. In the former, goddesses like Anat were being invoked alongside Yahweh, and there was some degree of Yahwist syncretism with the local Egyptian god Khnum. These documents show correspondence with Jerusalem, so it appears the Elephantine Jewish practices were kosher during that time period, please excuse the pun.

It seems monotheism came to the Jews rather late and probably imposed on the people top down from an intellectual elite; I'm looking squarely at the Maccabeans. Their revolt may have been like a full Bolshevik style revolution. As a Platonist of sorts I'm very sad to speculate that these revolutionaries may have been Plato fanboys in the wrong kind of way. They mixed ideas inspired by his political writings with a new type of religious feeling that was just beginning to sprout its wings in the Middle East.

So maybe a lot of those Psalms were leftovers from the Jews pre-monotheist era? I've seen other suggestions that speculate some of those Psalms are of Egyptian and Canaanite origin.

Secondly, do you think reverse engineering some of those Psalms back into a polytheist format would suck the magic out of them?

comments.title: Physical Culture and Magic
comments.content:
I have lately been very interested in the intersection of magic, spirituality, and what for lack of a better term I will call physical culture (defined as broadly as possible, including but not limited to strength training, martial arts, yoga, gymnastics, dance, and all movement practices). I've ben considering starting a Dreamwidth group to discuss the topic, but would first like to gauge the interest.

Does anyone have a physical practice that they feel complements their magical or spiritual practice well? Or would you like to figure out how to make the two work together?

I have many thoughts on how the physical side is not just a distraction, or even a balancing mechanism separate from the non-physical work. But for starters, I have long been into strength training and martial arts, without giving much attention to how they help in the development of the other stuff until recently. One historic example of someone who made the two work for him was Cabalist and strongman Joseph Greenstein, the Mighty Atom.

Currently I have a broad movement practice that includes a lot of different elements, and my martial art is Muay Thai. These don't interfere with my other practices at all, but beyond mere compatibility, I am looking for ways for each to inform the other and I think it's going quite well to begin with, though I have a lot left to explore. Please chime in if you have a practice or an interest in the matter.

replies.title: Re: Physical Culture and Magic
replies.content: I don't know how much of a contribution I'll have time to make, but this is a project well worth pursuing and I'll want to keep track of it. My main movement practice these days is an odd little offshoot of taijiquan called Tai Chi Chih -- it was invented by an American tai chi teacher named Justin Stone, and makes a good gentle exercise and qigong set -- but I've done a number of other practices over the years and I consider movement practices to be a valuable part of esoteric training.

replies.title: Re: Physical Culture and Magic

comments.title: A Speculation
comments.content:
A had a thought a while back that I just realized may be somewhat related to your vision/speculation about some souls from a higher cosmic plane getting themselves trapped on this one.

In every Indo-European mythology there are a group of beings — the titans, the fomorians, the jotunn, the asuras — which are not gods but are virtually indistinguishable from gods. Some of them intermarry with gods and have children, some of them straight up change sides and seem to become gods. Then at least in Greco-Roman and Germanic mythology, the creation story involves the chief of the gods (Zeus/Jupiter, Odin) killing the chief not-god (Cronus/Saturn, Ymir) before establishing the gods' reign over the Earth.

I wonder if this doesn't point to some disagreement between two groups of spirits from higher planes or previous phases of cosmic evolution: one who said "This is far enough, let's stop now and enjoy what we have," and another group that said, "No, the work isn't finished, let's keep going and creating." The latter group representing the gods, who fought with the first group and won. Having lost, some of the former group end up defecting to the gods' side, while others remain neutral-to-hostile to the gods' project.

This abstract story is now similar to the one told by the Gnostics, who simply take the first group's side in the conflict: the gods' sub-creation (to borrow a term from Tolkien) was necessarily messier and less perfect than the world the not-gods had inherited from their predecessors, and the Gnostics judge this bad.

It's also reminiscent of the Christian and Islamic myth of the War in Heaven: one party of angels said, "Let's stop, we're enough, no need to make these dirty little monkeys that'll just mess everything up," and the other party said, "No, the Divine Will is to keep going, and that's what we're going to do."

This is all deeply speculative, of course, and based on far too little knowledge of world religions to say that it's anything but a (hopefully clever) misreading of mythology. I just thought it might be worth putting out there.

replies.title: Re: A Speculation
replies.content: That's an intriguing parallel. Hmm.

replies.title: Re: A Speculation
replies.content:
I wonder how one might connect Ragnarök to that. Beings that have a foot in both groups deciding to go for a middle ground perhaps? "Sure, let's keep going but let's also throw out all the cruft and start some things over. Do we really need all these dirty little monkeys?"

—David P.

comments.title: The Uncanny Valley & Occult History
comments.content:
There's a phenomenon known as the uncanny valley, in which seeing something that looks and acts almost human, but not quite, causes a visceral reaction of disgust and horror. This has become more apparent in recent decades due to technology like CGI and lifelike robots, which have accidentally unlocked it.
Notably, this seems to be a deeply ingrained, instinctive reaction for humans. If you see something that's clearly just pretending to be human, it's not merely weird or creepy -- it's absolutely horrifying, and you want to kill and burn it.
Rationalist explanations include corpses, rabies in humans, and disease avoidance, and those may be contributing factors. But I can't help wondering if there's a better explanation on the occult side of things. Could the uncanny valley be related to zombies, or possession by evil spirits, or supernatural entities disguising themselves as human? Any info would be appreciated.

replies.title: Re: The Uncanny Valley & Occult History
replies.content: That's another fascinating point. I don't think it'll be based on zombies, because those are a culturally specific product of Haitian vodoun and equivalents are very uncommon. It's all but universal in folk traditions, though, that malign nonhuman spirits and hostile ghosts can make themselves look almost like human beings, so that's certainly a valid possibility. I'm also thinking here of the harrowing theory of Daniel Vendramini, that cannibalistic Neanderthals preyed on our species for millennia; if he's right, the Neanderthals would have looked almost human, but not quite -- and responding to them with horror and explosive violence would have been genetically ingrained on the surviving members of our species.

replies.title: Re: The Uncanny Valley & Occult History
replies.content: For that to work, it cannot be the appearence of Neanderthals that we find creepy (else we would react poorly to reconstructions of Neanderthal heads, and we don't), but the hypothetical mannerisms of them.

comments.title: If You Really Want It
comments.content:
What a very timely meme for me.

I've been an amateur writer for more than twenty years (in the directly translated sense of amateur, meaning 'lover') and have recently accomplished what I thought would be a big win for my relationship with my writing: a half-time, work-from-home promotion at one of my day jobs that would allow me to support us both (my writing and me) on a frugal budget while dedicating daily time for us to be alone together.

But something is not right. I think I can summarize it by describing my situation as one of those classic instances in intimate relationships: one partner, motivated to provide security and stability for both, pours their energy into career/money and ends up depleted, anxious, and having lost the will to find the simple everyday magic that the relationship requires. I know that relationships require work, but they also require romance, and your meme catches a bit of that truth quite well.

I'm proud of myself for having lined up a career goal and achieved it, but I get the feeling that my partner (my writing) is not as impressed as I'd hoped. In my struggle to buy us time together, I just haven't been making the time for us in small, ongoing ways. What a paradox! And I think there's an intimacy problem there that is worth journaling about.

This is less a question about occultism than a reflection on the love of writing, but I thought it would fit under this week's heading. Any comments or advice from the commentariat are welcome.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic: TV-watching is to metal cutlery as impulsive purchasing is to ___?
replies.content: The choice that comes first to mind is a tawny agate, a stone traditionally associated with the virtue of prudence. Wash it three times with cold water containing a little salt, dry it, and then place it in your cupped hands and, in your own words, ask it to help you stop impulse spending. Then carry it with you.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic: TV-watching is to metal cutlery as impulsive purchasing is to ___?
replies.content:
A more meditation or journaling - adjacent suggestion courtesy of Your Money or Your Life: Get a box or bag of some sort and clip a piece of paper to it. For every purchase you make, ensure you get a printed receipt for it if possible, or if it was an online purchase or you weren't able to get a physical receipt, write an itemized receipt out yourself by hand. When you get home or make an online purchase, add the total you spent to the paper and place the receipt in the bag.

At the end of the month, go through all your receipts. For each item ask yourself if the result was worth it. If not, add a "-" next to it. Total up the amount of money you spent on "-". Next month, do it all again.

This focuses your attention on what you are spending your money on and whether you have historically gotten satisfaction from your spending. This awareness exercise will over time help you stop and think "This was a - last time, why would I expect it to be worthwhile this time?" and refrain from spending.

I suppose this method could also work for reducing TV or other idle time as well - keep a list of what all you do each day and about how much time you spent on it. Mark "-" for the activities you feel were not worth the time spent on them. Then make another list of all the things you wanted to do but didn't get time to do and see how long it takes you to start noticing "I could be doing this other thing instead of being idle."

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I really like today's image! Being sensitive to my surroundings is a tough one for me. The good thing is that magic has made me tougher and more in control of that sensitivity.

Which reminds me. A commenter asked if sensitive people can do ceremonial magic and I wanted to reply but Saturn's relentless tick of time beat me. So here's my reply if that's alright and if the commenter happens to stumble upon it. You can do it, but there are some things to be careful with. The thing to take into consideration is that there are some things that will have to wait for you to do. But that is not a matter of a deficit, its a matter of most course works being made for people that need to work on their sensitivity first. We don't need that, and actually working on it first can be destabilizing, as the manual for the Hermetic Brotherhood of Luxor wisely points out in the opening chapter. If you already have enough sensitivity to experience basic psychic phenomena then the things to work on are discipline on the one hand, moderation on the other, and on the third hand. Oh never mind. I forgot we only have two hands. Those two are important because it's too easy to get distracted by the things that the basic practices will open up, and too easy to go out exploring. A little of that is necessary and fun for me to keep engaged but too much can make you a little looney, or psychically indigested. Hence the moderation and discipline. Very important. That last one is not only a metaphor. Psychic input must be processed by the rational mind, otherwise you might vomit it in burst of (interesting for sure) but unhealthy bursts of odd things. A little like going mad temporarily. Not recommended.

Madness, after all, is not much more than the uncontrollable opening up of the senses to the realities that surround us and the realities within us. Being too real all at once is uncomfortable because there are nasty things within us and around us. Scientists and doctors don't understand this so they can't help you. If you experience it, then its real, it doesn’t matter if somebody else doesn't experiences it, you are not writing a crude paper to agree with everyone else and get a grant. They work solely with the material world and haven't yet understood its multiplicity, so they are far behind on that one. Agreement is necessary in the material world only if you are going to build up something that everyone should use in the same way. The real question is if you are in control. If you are, then you are on your way to becoming a magician, if you are not, you are a psychiatric case. If you are up a mountain, the people on the ground won't believe the things up there, it’s not in their experience, so they will call you names and push you aside. The way to get control over those, if you care about your daily working mind that is, and one should, is to open it up a little, meditate and reflect on what you saw and experienced and slowly building up a coherent and ordered vision of things. It doesn't have to be an ecstatic experience, even integrating an emotional trauma can be destabilizing and needs to be made to fit the way you see and assemble the world, otherwise your world can get fragmented. Otherwise you might get all jumbled up and you start loosing track of what is what. In a much more extreme case, that is what happens to schizophrenics for example, they can't add up their inner and outer, so they end up splitting. A serious tizzy in resumed counts and if you are like me, a tizzy feels good and makes you feel alive but that is because psychic phenomena acts on the brain the same way as drugs do. And as drugs there is such a thing as intoxication. So, moderation and discipline. Saturn and Mars are your friends here.

There is a bright side to all this too. One part is that one needs much less to contact the subtle realities that magicians explore. So it is not a matter of feeling like you can't do some stuff, it's a matter of you not needing right now that stuff to achieve the same or more intense effects. So in my practice for example, instead of intense rituals to open up yourself to get in contact with say, a god or goddess, I need a much simpler and less intense ritual backed up with prayer and meditation to do it. It has happened to me that someone does an intense ritual and nothing happens to them but it's I who is all blissed out on a chair nearby. That kind of thing. So prayer is a powerful tool, prayer is the end tool in some way because at some point all becomes mind and you only need to talk; prayer is talking, and talking and listening in relationship with the divine is meditation. Or is it prayer? Meditation is prayer once you recognize the divine aspects of thought and everything, and prayer is meditation once you build up your inner muscles to climb to those aspects of thought.

Eventually, once sensitivity is under control, more intensive ritual can be undertaken if one wants to. Anyways, a few notes on sensitivity and ceremonial magic for you all. Oh I remembered the third hand, because at some point your sprout a third hand, didn't you know? Physical exercise. It has to be the kind that hurts, or at least that one works the best. A little walk or jog won't do it. The reason for it is that the only distinction between the physical worlds and the sensible worlds is where you put your attention. It’s attention ultimately that does the trick, since both the buttered bagel and the steaming cup of tea next to it are right there overlapping with the cosmic creative drumming of The Highest One. Both are right here right now in the same place. Pay attention to either and it's the one you resonate with and start experiencing and shaping your reality. Realize that both are one and the same, that's enlightenment, or so they say. So why painful exercise? Because pain takes your attention straight back into your body, no questions asked. Now you know why some people undergoing serious mental stress cut themselves. Unknowingly that is what they are doing, taking away their attention from the mess their minds have become (and as such the world they inhabit) and they feel more grounded. There are better ways that do that, that do not involve self depreciation and breaking the body but rather that builds it up and thus it becomes more efficient. So weight lifting, burpees, pushups, chinups, martial arts or stuff like that work very well. If it makes you sweat and tenses your muscles, that is good enough. Anyway, a little long but hope it is helpful if someone is in the same situation I was 9 years ago. My gods, 9 years already! Have I been rambling in the corner of the internet for 9 years? Thanks for putting up with a little madness for so long JMG and everyone :-).

Anyway, I did have a question today.

Today I decided to walk instead of taking a taxi, for as much as it was the most polite offer for a taxi I have ever been given, from the Narita airport to the hotel 35 minutes away. I was strolling and musing as I sometimes do, taking in a new air and the very different world that Japan is, step by step. It was a shinto shrine to Ōkami Inari, god of swordsmiths, agriculture and foxes. While doing that I started noticing the spirits of the place and they felt, well, very Japanese. Thank you Ōkami Inari, I am just passing by today but will say hi more thoroughly on my way back. I am on my way to southern India right now to intern myself on an ashram with Sadhguru for three months, as some of us crazy ones sometimes do. Wish me luck!

This whole thing of world peace and ending war strikes me as hopelessly naïve. The problem of evil is solved by free will, and any species with aggression & free will is going to fight wars. Thus, ending wars would mean removing aggression and/or free will; which in turn would lead to a dystopian nightmare, with humans enslaved by a totalitarian one world government, with chains & fetters on our very brains and genetics. I don't know about you, but I'd much prefer the sudden onslaught of war to the slow poison of slavery & bondage.

And that's the fatal flaw with John Lennon's Imagine -- in a world where there's nothing to kill or die for, there's nothing to live for either. Meaning, purpose, and value are fundamental to human existence (in fact, the Archdruid has identified this as the true path to enlightenment), and war, for all its horror, plays a role in this.

War is ultimately Darwinian, a pressure test for societies to compete in a life & death struggle, so that the fit attain victory and the unfit go down in defeat. Take the Russo-Ukrainian War, for instance -- it's terrible, but it's also part of the downfall of a thoroughly corrupt, rapacious elite class, and Russian victory there will pave the way towards a better, multipolar future.

This ties into a larger tension between Darwinian morality versus herd morality -- we need both, and either one can be taken too far. Thus, we need both war & peace (there's more I could say on this, but I'll leave it there).

This should help you understand that there are reasons for why human life is the way it is -- the good, bad & ugly; the light and the darkness. Maybe none of us have the wisdom to foresee where meddling with human nature would end up, and thus have no business beseeching higher powers to alter their judgment.

1) Humans clearly don't actually need physical light to visually observe things and places—scrying, visionary dreams, werewolves (?), etc. prove that. I'm wondering whether it's possible to make use of that in everyday life: if it gets too dark, just close your eyes and see.

comments.title: Relocation Checklist
comments.content:
jmg

In an earlier thread you mentioned you may be looking into relocation (may you find solace).

My question is how would a mage go about deciding where to relocate to? I know of Relocation Astrology and guessing you would use that but wondering if you have any other "tools" or items on a "Relocation checklist" you would recommend?

For me, I know of Relocation Astrology and have online tools for that and have had a relocation chart done up last year. I get reasonably good results from card divination. I'd guess one should also feel some sort of interest in a place and it's history and / or geography, flora & fauna as these would point towards compatibility with local gods.

replies.title: Re: Relocation Checklist
replies.content:
I start in a very mundane way by making a list of the things that I want in a new place. I then go looking to find places that have those things; the internet makes that easier than it used to be. While I'm doing this I also pay close attention to intuition, which will quite often pull me toward some places and push me away from others.

Once I have a short list, I do relocation charts for each place. The style I use is from Llewellyn George's books; it involves casting your birth chart as though you were born at the same moment but in the place you're thinking of moving. The planets are all in the same signs and degrees of the zodiac, but their house placements can change considerably. That way you can rotate benefics into angles and push malefics into houses where they won't bother you. (That's why I live on the east coast -- on the west coast, Saturn's in an angle and several other planets are in bad positions relative to my career, while on the east coast Saturn's weakly placed and I've got a benefic conjunct my midheaven.)

comments.title: Time Magic & Energy Center work
comments.content:
Good evening! I've got a query about magic, and this happens to be just the right place to receive knowledge on this matter. I have two questions on this fine Magic Monday.

Firstly: I practiced daily LBRP and middle piller for some time and dabbled with some other Golden Dawn stuff. Whilst Ive found it quite effective, there's something about Golden Dawn system that feels a bit off for me personally. It feels like I'm intruding in someone else's tradition, using the Kabbalah and not being Jewish and all that. I'm looking to start with the SoP soon instead. My question is: is there any practice I can combine with SoP that works the inner energy centers, in similar fashion to middle pillar which I've found the highly beneficial? Also, the planetary workings in Golden Dawn quite appeal to me. Are there other kinds of planetary workings that can be combined with SoP? And would I be right to assume that one can't just cherry pick some practices from a tradition without grounding them in a complete routine of that tradition?

My second question concerns the question of 'time' in magical practice. What makes astrology so distinctively interesting is that it provides a framework for mapping one's subjective experiences onto the passing of time, so that qualitatively distinct time periods and cycles become distinguishable. Whilst astrology is highly useful in this way, it strikes me that it doesn't provide all the answers. E.g. some time periods feel more 'magical' than others, whilst astrology does not indicate this. To me, a big part of my practice is learning to recognize, and respond to, the quality of time that I am experiencing.

Now my question: are there, besides astrology, other magical tools, workings, concepts etc. that help the magician to navigate this passing of time?

An example: whilst the daily practice is an important cornerstone of magical practice, I find that weekly and monthly routines become just as important. Some periods resemble former cycles experienced years ago and form recurring patterns. At other times, it feels like magic is not in the air at all, and it is just a time of focusing on mundane earthly matters. Perhaps the moon cycles help here... But it feels like there could be more magical tools beyond astrology here, to help recognize and respond to particular (novel or recurring) qualities of time.

replies.content: I think once you have the basics of scrying, the best way to improve your skill is to practice scrying regularly.

replies.title: Re: Sharpening my scrying skills
replies.content:
Ada Goodrich-Freer (writing as "Miss X") published a number of truly excellent articles on scrying way back in the late 1800s:
(1) "Recent Experiments in Crystal-Vision.” Proceedings of the Society of Psychical Research, 5 (1888-1889), pp. 486-521.
(2) “On the Faculty of Crystal Gazing.” Pages 103-141 in her Essays in Psychical Research, London: George Redway, 1899.
(3) "How It Came into My Head – The Machinery of Intuitions." Pages 169-210 in the same Essays in Psychical Research.

Theodore Besterman's book, Crystal-Gazing: A Study in the History, Distribution, Theory and Practice of Scrying (London: Rider, 1924), is also excellent.

More recently, Raymond Moody (the writer on near-death experiences) published a very interesting short pamphlet, Scrying: The Art of Female Divination (1999). Moody's book Reunions (1993) has a fascinating discussion of a scrying chamber that he designed, which he calls a Psychomanteum.

The late Christian magician/mystic Arthur Pigg, who wrote under the pseudonym Ambrose Hawk and once had a valuable website, published Exploring Scrying: How to Divine the Future and Make the Most of It (New Page Books, 2001).

There are more books are articles that I might add to this list, but these should give you plenty to get started with.

You can find them all online with the possible exception of Ambrose Hawk's book.

The plural, oddly enough, is "ephemerides."

I use the Rosicrucian Ephemeris, which is published by the Rosicrucian Fellowship in California. You can get print copies here:

https://www.rosicrucian.com/bookstore/index.html#astro

https://astrologers.com/authors/rosicrucian/

comments.title: Tarot Systems, Symbolism and Correspondences
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

my wife shows an interest in taking up a 'light' version of occult study and practice - 'light' mainly due to time constraints. After talking about it for some time she now plans to - in a first step - take up the practice of the SoP and the daily three card reading using the RWS Tarot as outlined in the FHR curriculum.

The free format of going by the LWB and her own intuition however doesn't sit quite right with her. She would appreciate some more structure and guidance.

I have told her a lot about what I know about the classic Golden Dawn system of correspondences prior to this (which likely shaped her understanding of the endeavor) and was thus on the lookout for some (German) material in that general direction that still doesn't go too far down the deep end. (It's still supposed to be 'occultism light'.)

This got me thinking about this general question that I return to again and again:

How should one sensibly go about learning the Tarot? A strict system of correspondences (as in Liber 777)? Or basing all on the image, the feeling and the personal intuition of card and reading? Some combination of both? But how to weigh that? What to use as the base system? Etc.

Years back, when I first learned the Tarot using Crowley's Book of Thoth I got to appreciate the beauty and richness of that system and I still have trouble to imagine how anyone would (want) start to learn the tarot by merely looking at the pictures of the cards and going by their intuition.

On the other hand I can see that (a lot of) people do in fact do exactly that and seem to get (good) results with it.

So, I guess, my questions are:

1) What would you advise? Is it a personal matter?

2) What ressource(s) would you point someone to who has trouble with this freeform approach? (Might John Gilbert's The Doors of Tarot be just what I/we are looking for?)

Thank you!

- Florian

replies.title: Re: Tarot Systems, Symbolism and Correspondences
replies.content: It's a personal matter, but there are ways to make it easier and also a lot of middle ground. Yes, I would recommend John's The Doors of Tarot -- that book is based on his tarot classes, which took complete beginners and had them reading tarot easily and making accurate predictions after a single day.

replies.title: Re: Tarot Systems, Symbolism and Correspondences
replies.content:
Alright, thank you!

I'll get her a copy then.

comments.title: the goodies-from-spirits mentality
comments.content:
I've been reflecting further about the anon who posted above talking about summoning the archangel Metatron and getting him to end war, and realized that it's yet another example of the mentality that treats spiritual beings as vending machines from which human beings can get goodies on request. It's not just that that's what the anon was asking about -- there's a weird mentality that shows up reliably with such people (and was the reason that I ban such questions generally). They don't engage in dialogue. If what I say doesn't fit into their expectations, they just look blank, ignore everything that I've said, and rabbit on about getting this or that goodie from this or that spirit. If they disagreed with me and said so, that would be fine, but they don't -- they just talk past anything that doesn't support their fixation.

I've seen that same mentality before, in one other context. Point out to true believers in nuclear power, space travel, etc. that technical feasibility doesn't matter if the economics don't work and you'll get an identical response. I once lost my cool on my blog and more or less started shouting at people because I posted an entire essay exploring that weird reaction in so many words, and a whole flurry of people who posted comments just babbled about technical feasibility as though I'd never said a word about economics.

I'm not sure why this bizarre sort of nonthinking shows up so reliably in these two subjects. I'm deeply concerned that what we're seeing here is a form of mental failure -- not the specific model of cognitive collapse I plan on discussing in an upcoming blog post, but a similar implosion of the ability to think that leads to a weirdly reflexive mouthing of canned talking points. It reminds me, weirdly enough, of autism -- and of course I say this as a person with autism! Are there patterns of cognition that can create a kind of single-issue autism?

replies.title: Re: the goodies-from-spirits mentality
replies.content:
("No! This cannot be! I am invincibllllleeeeeeee!!!")

There's this recently trendy idea in cognitive psychology called the predictive-processing model. It (at least when interpreted charitably) proposes that a major part of how instinct steers behavior to achieve desired outcomes is:

(1) The brain develops a purely-predictive model of one's possible actions and resulting possible experiences, made of a web of a bunch of probabilistic dependencies like "if event X happens, then event Y has thus-and-such increased amount of vague intuitive increased probabilistic impetus to happen, compared to if event X doesn't happen". This is a relatively easy process to set up, because it's mostly just gathering statistics about actually-observed things.

(2) (But part of the secret sauce that will be needed later on for the whole system to work is that, during this step, the brain has to engage in the right kind of figuration of bundling together concrete observations into abstract observations. Especially observations about one's actions; the space of possible combinations of concrete low-level actions has to be divvied up in particular ways, and the representation of future actions and scenarios of gathering future information has to be done in a particular way or the whole thing becomes dysfunctional.)

(3) The instincts involved in preference and reward then warp that model-web with constraints like, "Suppose instinct-imposed goal G was much more likely than would have been expected, for, like, no reason at all". Or even, "Suppose goal G was guaranteed, out of nowhere, to happen".

(4) At that point, the instincts can then make a probabilistic query: "In that hypothetical range of scenarios with that added constraint, which actions would have been unexpectedly likely to happen as the path-of-least-resistance for goal G to happen?". And then those are the actions that pop out as actual behavior.

It's actually relatively mathematically more natural to emergently set up a neural system to calculate all of that, than to do the harder work where you imagine full scenarios without mysterious future guarantees of success and then look at the chances of success that are led to by each possible action. The technical term for the shortcut form of cognitive work here is "planning by inference": you probabilistically infer what your plan might have had to be, given the constraint that you succeed in the end.

But the big problem with planning by inference is that it has no built-in way to distinguish skill from luck, or prudent investigation from correct guessing. You can sort of make it work with weird workarounds, like the secret sauce from step (2), or using a reorganized picture of the world where, for example, the universe actively punishes "imprudence" with weird probability distortions to make you fail. But these workarounds aren't very effective outside of scenarios where you have a lot of practice or experience or remembered lore and wisdom.

As a partly overlapping problem, predictive-processing is vulnerable to pathologies where different probability-constraint signals propagating through the web of dependencies become too strong or too weak. Delirium and psychosis sort of correspond to bottom-up feedback signals that restrict plausibility being too weak. Depression sort of corresponds to all the signals being too weak. Mania sort of corresponds to the top-down signals about being guaranteed to get what you want being too strong compared to the bottom-up signals that restrict plausibility, or other top-down signals corresponding to other peoples' strategies to refuse things. Maybe dementia corresponds to signals from middle levels being too strong relative to other signals -- rote patterns with too much probabilistic-impetus compared to bottom-up signals about plausibility and realistic diversity, or top-down signals about relevant context.

If a man has an instinct stomping real hard on a button that looks like "some kind of romantic partnership attempt will definitely succeed, this is the most important constraint in the entire system of the future", and the other parts of the system aren't there to restrain and direct that force, then it could totally make sense to see dysfunctions like that.

replies.title: Re: the goodies-from-spirits mentality
replies.content:
I was going to reply under the top-level, but your comment actually covers a lot of what I wanted to say better than I would have said it.

Some other parts:

In retrospect, it would only make sense that narratives about the future and about one's potential connections with it are part of one's narrative identity, and as such, are able to cause threats to those narratives to be experienced as as a threat to one's identity. One's instincts presumably have a strong incentive to be able to keep their finger on the pulse of, "so, this representation in thus-and-such brain region that refers to 'me', that is, to the life of the organism whose bloodline this is -- does that representation of an entity have a place within that same brain's representation of the future, that is, does the brain expect the organism to be alive and in a good position?", so as to be able to conveniently attach the fear of death to any hint of a prospect of "oneself" no longer recognizably existing.

For example, Zen practice is known for sometimes doing away with the fear of death as such. And one common side-effect of Zen practice is that it disrupts one's ability to form or recall episodic memories -- that is, a certain kind of narrative memories about oneself -- from past the point of kenshö. These would make sense together if one thing Zen practice did is disrupt the normal instinctively compulsive constant production of a self-representation of the sort that one's instincts knew how to do things like attach a fear of death to. (Or a narcissistic delusory automatic self-favoring bias in evaluation, or a concern for one's social position, or...)

Also, for some reason people in exotic psychological states keep having experiences for which they find the term "ego death" natural, so that they immediately use that term to describe that experience if they know the term exists. And why otherwise would that happen, out of nowhere? It's not like there was anything obvious that was actually dying.

Another instinct that might train its attention upon such narratives about the future and about one's potential connections with it is the instinct for being perceived favorably in society. If instinct can find a way to pull a person's strings so as to cause the person to have distorted representations about how they or their bloodline deserve a given favorable place in the world in the future, then the instinct is rewarded genetically by giving the person a way to "lie without (detectably) lying" about how the only reasonable outcome is for for other people to cede that place to them, thus making that the convenient starting point for social haggling about what place they will actually get. (This business about "deserving a place" is also part of how narcissistic and borderline personality disorders work: narcissistic by projecting that image about oneself, and borderline by receiving and reinforcing such an image by one's relation to someone else, especially a narcissist.) More innocently, this is also how instinct can organize motivations for the person to actually do things that other people find valuable, so as to make those other people more likely to cede the preferred place in society to that person (even in the absence of haggling): just make the person care about whether or not their actions will realistically make other people feel more favorably about narratively-represented futures that assign a more favorable place to to the person.

So, fear of the destruction of one's entire image about the future probably pulls a lot of the same strings as (a) the fear of death, (b) the fear of being kicked out of one's place in society, and to some extent even (c) the fear of "objectively" not being adequately valuable as a person.

and, separately:

In Yudkowsky-Alexander-and-in-this-case-also-Hanson rationality-cultivation culture, we find a distinction between one category of "beliefs as attire" or "luxury beliefs", and another category of beliefs that "pay rent" (by sticking their necks out to constrain what kinds of observations one might expect to make under various circumstances if the beliefs had been true, and getting cut off to various degrees if those observations don't later obligingly manifest). To illustrate the distinction, imagine a fuzzy belief about what might be possible in the future, and imagine that you're making a bet about the future and trying to work out the odds at which you would switch over from wanting to take one side of the bet to wanting to take the other side. Even just an imaginary focus on that kind of personally-dear material consequence produces a very different frame of mind, where many of the considerations that might normally affect what belief you think yourself to have fall away. More on the theory of this here:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/336984.html?thread=56557912#cmt56614744

Identity beliefs, as well as weirdly distortionary beliefs about the future, are prooobably in the category of "beliefs as attire", in that scheme.

This is part of the reason for our liking Paul Graham's maxim of "keep your identity small". (The natural extension to "keep your investment in narratives about the future and your place in it small" is not quite so compactly or coherently articulated, although Yudkowsky has occasionally tried to advance a rubric of virtues one of which is "lightness".)

Supposedly you can also temporarily (like for 30 seconds' duration) be rid of some kinds of floating out-of-place beliefs by abruptly squirting ice-cold water in your ear in a particular position to trigger some kind of vestibulo-ocular reflex, maybe because your brain is doing some kind of emergency response that requires stronger communication between regions that don't normally communicate as much.

The strikingness of the disconnect in relation to specifically nuclear power might be because of the thing with the "as low as reasonably possible"/"as low as reasonably achievable" regulatory standard, which distorts the economics of nuclear power in the USA.

Your side might, when estimating the EROEI-style cost of nuclear power, include estimates averaged over American nuclear projects and conclude that these estimates indicate that it's unsustainable. Whereas the other side might think that, because of the machinations of the fossil fuel lobby, American nuclear power is effectively (though obscurely and deniably) required by law to not be foreseeably profitable on average, because "reasonable" is by design interpreted to mean "keep adding features during the design stage until you can't make the leaked quantities and exposures any lower without making it unprofitable", and therefore the American experience of expense of nuclear projects shouldn't be taken into account.

comments.title: Intuition?
comments.content:
Dear JMG and fellow Monday Magicians’, ok, MM readers 😊,

I have a comment and a question about a certain little game I’ve been playing with myself last three months or so.
I have a bag of dental treats for my dog, and there are three colours: white, green and red. I ask myself: “What colour am I going to take out today?” And out of the 20 or so answers I got only 3 wrong; and that is, I believe, because I was not listening carefully and got confused by rational thinking (combinatorics; say, the original answer was white and I was thinking, I drew 4 white in a row so it cannot be…but sure it was).
I would like to ask: Do little games like this one help develop intuition?
And why, whenever I ask the question, the right answer is the first but the quietest and I feel a little uncomfortable about this answer? It is interesting how loud then my usual thinking becomes, compared to this initial answer, so I must be really perceptive and also not to give in to the later – and louder – suggestions.

Thank you.
Have a nice day and my blessings, if welcome.
With regards,
Markéta

Children vary sharply in readiness for mathematics, and if you try to force them to learn it before they're ready, they can end up so stressed about it that it will prevent them from learning it well later. You might look up information about readiness factors in intellectual development; it's been decades since I looked into this, or I'd point you to some sources.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: In support of this: math was my worst subject in K-12 and I struggled mightily with it in college despite getting a degree that required I take a good many math classes. Since then something about it clicked and I have caught up quite a bit on the math I struggled most with or missed out on all those years ago.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's very common. I once read of an entire class of kids who, as an experiment, were taught no math at all until 6th grade. By halfway through the year they were all past grade level. The role of readiness factors in education is badly neglected in our assembly-line schooling system.

replies.title: learning math
replies.content: In addition to trying to teach concepts the child may or may not be ready for, some books and ways of teaching math are not conducive to the best ways for most children to learn. There are alot of different ways to teach math.  There are alot of math books that would have had my youngest ( who homeschooled ) in tears more likely she would have just chucked it out the door.  I personally do not think a set book or set of worksheets, etc.. is ever appropriate for young children, and you do not say what age or level the child is. It was some work to tailor the approaches to the particular child I had, but there are realy so, so many ways and so so many approaches and resources, but the best ones were more what I came up with that were from no book or approach that I read about.  I learned alot by being ready to answer questions for this child, I can now make a logical reasoning why dividing fractions has this shortcut we all use that works and is convenient, but it is not realy a thing to be just told what to do as then we dont know why or how this is logically consistent with how we understand the process of division. Then younger children needs stories and imagination and hands on in most cases and some connection to their liffe, were they are at how they think about things

comments.title: Medieval and Early Modern Exorcism booklets
comments.content:
Here's a list in chronological order of several Latin books of prayers printed for personal use against psychic or spiritual attack:

Coniuratio malignorum spirituum / Coniuratio daemonum. [1477? and very many later editions.]

Oratione dil devoto santo Cypriano. [1500?]

Exorcismi contra dæmoniacos diversorum sanctorum approbati cum benedictionibus necessariis. 1513.

Orationes devotæ pro infirmis et in agone constitutis. 1516.

Exorcismo mirabile & bono da disfare ogni sorte maleficii. [ca. 1555.]

Thesaurus exorcismorum. 1608.

Thesaurus exorcismorum. 1626.

I have been able to find all of these books as PDFs here and there on the internet, though sometimes only from European rare-book library sites.

The first of them (Coniuratio malignorum spirituum /Coniuratio daemonum) was by far the most popular and best selling of the lot. It's a short little booklet, only 20 pages long in its first edition. The later titles in this list are generally much longer.

A English translation of it was published by Trident Books in 1998 as an appendix (pp. 65-81) to its book, The Great Grimoire of Pope Honorius.

Also, another member of the commentariat mentioned Psalm 91. A very insightful historian of American religions (and, incidentally, of Lovecraft-style literary horror) named Philip Jenkins recently published an entire book on that Psalm's use as a means of protection against spiritual attack. Its title is He Will Save You from the Deadly Pestilence: The Many Lives of Psalm 91.

replies.title: Re: Medieval and Early Modern Exorcism booklets
replies.content:
Hi Robert,

replies.title: Re: Medieval and Early Modern Exorcism booklets
replies.content: Most of the others are fairly hefty books, and I wouldn't expect to find them ever translated into English. They were all printed for practicing Roman Catholics, which has some bearing on whether there might have been any market for an English translation at the time they were published. (Also, two of them are in Italian, not Latin: the second in the list and the fifth.)

comments.title: Demonolatry Books
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

I got caught up in Demonolatry several years ago. I’m no longer involved and asked deities to sever any connections to demonic entities, which seems to have been successful.

I still have some Demonolatry books in my possession. I’m thinking I should get rid of them, but selling them brings up an ethical question of whether that would be helping to lead someone else down a dark path. Would it be better to somehow dispose of them?

replies.title: Re: Demonolatry Books
replies.content: I rarely advise disposing of books, but there are exceptions. On the rare occasions that I end up with a book that I think deserves disposal, I break it open (splitting the spine into several chunks, each with some pages attached) and throw it in the kitchen trash, where wet tea leaves and soggy scraps await them. You might consider something of the kind.
